query: severe itching finger webspace burrows scabies

retrieved_docs.title: Insect Bites -- Etiology -- Insecta
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies is an infestation of the skin caused by the 8-legged human itch mite, Sarcoptes scabiei . The mite is small, around 0.4mm, and burrows into the stratum corneum layer of skin where it lays its eggs. Scabies has a worldwide distribution and usually spread through prolonged direct contact, often sexual. Initial infections are often asymptomatic for 2 to 6 weeks, and the development of symptoms is the result of an allergic response to mite proteins deposited under the skin. [4] The most common manifestations of scabies infestation include intense itching, often worse at night or with exposure to warm temperatures, and the presence of erythematous papules or nodules. Tiny burrows created by the tunneling of the female mite under the skin may appear as raised, linear or curved grayish-white lines on the skin surface, and sometimes contain a black speck representing the female mite. Cutaneous vesicles may be present but are a more common presentation of scabies infestation in infants and young children. The dermatologic findings in scabies infestations most commonly present in the web spaces of the fingers and toes, ventral surface of the wrist, elbows, back, buttocks and external genitals. The diagnostic basis is on symptoms of intense pruritis accompanied by the appearance of a corresponding rash or burrows. Confirmation of the diagnosis can is by microscopic evaluation of a skin scraping revealing the mite, its eggs or fecal material. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies is a contagious skin condition caused by the mite Sarcoptes scabiei which burrows into the skin and causes severe itching. Scabies is transmitted by direct skin-to-skin contact or indirectly by contact with contaminated material (fomites). This condition is often challenging to diagnose as many patients may have only subtle symptoms. However, other patients may present with the classic history of exposure, severe pruritis that is worse at night, and reference to other individuals with similar symptoms. This activity reviews the evaluation and treatment of scabies and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in managing patients with this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment failure is common, and isolating the cause can help prevent further infection and limit outbreaks in communities.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The classic form of scabies may have a population of mites on an individual that range between 10 to 15 organisms. It typically takes ten minutes of skin-to-skin contact for mites to transmit to another human host, in cases of classic scabies. Transmission of the disease can also occur by fomite transmission via clothing or bed sheets.  This presentation of scabies often manifests with hyperkeratotic plaques that can be diffuse or localized to the palms, soles, and under fingernails.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Examination findings include serpiginous white lines indicating mite burrowing. Common sites of mite burrowing include intertriginous areas, axillae, umbilicus, between digits, the beltline, nipples, buttocks, areolae of female breasts, flexural surfaces of the wrists or on the penile shaft. Type IV hypersensitivity reactions to the mite, eggs, or excrement may occur, forming erythematous papules. The itching associated with scabies gives way to scratching, encrustation, and possible impetiginization [6] [4] .

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Not all individuals exhibit classic manifestations of scabies infestations, which can make the infection challenging to diagnose. Patients may only have subtle signs and may not demonstrate the typical clues, which include a history of exposure, severe pruritis at night, or close contacts with a similar presentation [6] .   Pruritis among several family members or close contacts should always cause the provider to think of scabies.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Treating the patient along with close contacts and family members is associated with a good prognosis. With adequate treatment, patients can be expected to recover fully. Without treatment, the infection can spread to other members of the community and cause an outbreak within the population.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sarcoptes scabiei resides in the dermal and epidermal layers of humans as well as animals. Scabies occurs worldwide and is a common skin condition. The infestation begins with the female mite burrowing within the stratum corneum of its host where it lays its eggs. It later develops into larvae, nymphs, and adults.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Adult female mites dig burrow tunnels 1 to 10 millimeters long within the superficial layers of the epidermis and lay 2 to 3 eggs daily. The mites die 30 to 60 days later, and the eggs hatch after approximately 2 to 3 weeks. It merits mentioning that not all treatment options can penetrate the eggs stored within the skin [2] [7] .

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Possible complications of a scabies infection include persistent itching, insomnia, secondary bacterial infection, and outbreaks of the disease to the community [6] .

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Recognizing scabies infection and being able to diagnose this condition is crucial for patient care. One study showed that 45% of patients diagnosed with scabies, were actually suffering from other conditions such as eczema, papular dermatitis, irritant dermatitis, or contact dermatitis.

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies is a contagious skin condition resulting from the infestation of a mite. The Sarcoptes scabiei mite burrows within the skin and causes severe itching.  This itch is relentless, especially at night. Skin-to-skin contact transmits the infectious organism therefore, family members and skin contact relationships create the highest risk.  Scabies was declared a neglected skin disease by the World Health Organization (WHO) in 2009 and is a significant health concern in many developing countries. Infested individuals require identification and prompt treatment because a misdiagnosis can lead to outbreaks,  morbidity, and an increased economic burden [1] .

retrieved_docs.title: Permethrin -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: A patient with classic scabies often presents with generalized pruritus that is exacerbated overnight with inflammatory papules primarily localized to the area between the fingers, flexural sites, genitalia, breasts, and buttocks with or without burrows (see Images. Rash and Scabies sites, Scabies Burrows, Scabies Scaly Patch). The clinical diagnosis of pediculosis capitis relies upon the detection of living lice (see Images. Crusted Scabies, Scabies in Skin Scraping.). [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Scabies -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Scabies is a significant problem in public health, and one common reason for treatment failure is non-adherence to the treatment regimen. Additionally, improvements in living conditions, avoidance of overcrowding, and avoidance of sharing personal care products can decrease the likelihood of persistence of this pest. While some individuals do obtain relief from symptoms, recurrences are frequent. The level of interprofessional collaboration outlined above is critical to treatment success and optimal outcomes. [Level 5]

retrieved_docs.title: Insect Bites -- Etiology -- Insecta
retrieved_docs.content: Simple, uncomplicated tick bites are treated with routine wound care, topical corticosteroids and systemic antihistamines for pruritic lesions and antibiotics if secondary infection is present. Ticks should be removed with fine-tipped tweezers, grasping the tick as close to the skin as possible and pulling upward with steady, gentle pressure. Other commonly touted methods of tick removal such as applying fingernail polish, alcohol or a hot extinguished match are not recommended as they do not affect detachment and may cause the tick to regurgitate into the wound, increasing the risk of disease transmission. [6] Scabies

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2639
retrieved_docs.content: Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides are classified as a type of ectoparasiticides, a group that also includes scabicides. As sulfur‑based agents, these ectoparasiticides are employed to treat infestations caused by external parasites such as mites, lice, and scabies‑causing organisms, leveraging sulfur’s antiparasitic properties to eliminate or control the offending organisms and alleviate associated skin irritation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2434
retrieved_docs.content: Other ectoparasiticides, including scabicides in ATC, are classified as a subset of ectoparasiticides, including scabicides.

retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 555
retrieved_docs.content: Phenothrin is a synthetic pyrethroid that belongs to the broader class of pyrethrines, a group of synthetic compounds commonly employed as ectoparasiticides and scabicides. As a member of the pyrethrines, phenothrin functions as a pesticide, targeting external parasites such as lice, mites, and the organisms that cause scabies. This classification highlights phenothrin’s role within the pyrethrine family of ectoparasiticides and underscores its utility in pest control and the treatment of parasitic skin conditions.

SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS; the latter represent a specific subgroup of ant parasitic agents that target ectoparasites and include scabicides, insecticides, and repellents.

Dapsone is a member of the drug class used for the treatment of leprosy and is also employed in managing herpetic skin inflammation, illustrating its utility in both chronic bacterial infection and certain viral‑induced dermatologic conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2600
retrieved_docs.content: Yersinia pestis, the bacterial pathogen responsible for plague, is associated with infections of the skin and subcutaneous tissues, manifesting as lesions such as buboes that reflect the organism’s ability to invade and proliferate within these superficial layers. Recognizing this link is important for clinicians when evaluating patients with cutaneous or subcutaneous swellings, especially in endemic areas or after exposure risks, to ensure prompt diagnosis, appropriate antimicrobial therapy, and infection‑control measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1642
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIFUNGALS FOR DERMATOLOGICAL USE constitute a broad class of antifungal agents employed in the treatment of skin disorders, and within this class, antifungals for topical use represent the specific subset of medications applied directly to the skin surface to manage superficial fungal infections. Recognizing that topical antifungals are a component of the larger dermatological antifungal category helps clinicians select appropriate therapy based on the site and severity of infection.

retrieved_docs.content: Efinaconazole, fenticonazole, flutrimazole, and isoconazole are all members of the imidazole and triazole derivatives class of topical antifungal agents, sharing a common chemical scaffold that underlies their use in treating superficial fungal infections.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 553
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIPARASITIC PRODUCTS, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS is the broad category that includes the subgroup ECTOPARASITICIDES, INCL. SCABICIDES, INSECTICIDES AND REPELLENTS. Within this subgroup, the class ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides is contained, and Sulfur containing product ectoparasiticides represent a specific type of ectoparasiticides, incl. scabicides. This hierarchical organization clarifies how the general anti‑parasitic product category is subdivided into increasingly specific ectoparasiticidal agents, with sulfur‑based formulations constituting a particular subclass, aiding in classification, regulation, and appropriate selection of treatments for ectoparasite control.
